sentences.text: From '41 till '40-- till the war finished.
So I think '44 or '45, I don't remember.
Three years, I think.
Yeah.
Nobody knew.
Just one-- after, when I have been notified that my father got killed and I was crying.
And I had the mother superior for the young people-- called [UKRAINIAN] in Ukrainian, I don't know in English-- she was so nice to me, like a mother.
So when I cried, I did tell her the truth.
I tell her, I do like Jesus very much, because you-- probably his wish was me to be alive.
Only the true why I cry was because I heard that my father and my brother got killed.
And I really miss them, and I cannot lie.
I never brought up lying.
And I have to tell you the truth-- I am Jewish and I want you to know.
And she was so nice to me.
She really liked me.
She said, look.
You don't have to be a nun.
You can be a Christian, and go your way, and do what you want to do.
And that's why I felt to do big support for her.
I want to support them because, after the war, there was ammunition hidden in that church against the Polish people, because Ukrainian didn't like Polish.
The [INAUDIBLE] is so big [LAUGHS] that we don't know really where to get.
So the Ukrainian, my old, beautiful nuns had to be sent to Siberia because the Russian didn't like to see so many ammunition in a church.
Because church should be only for God, not for politic.
And I have been mixed in very strong political.
Why?
I didn't like the Polish.
They want Lwow to belong to Ukraine.
And when they decide to send the nuns to Siberia, old girls, and Rabbi Kahane and the wife, we all had a big list to support them.
Only they have been the same way like the Germans, because they didn't listen to us.
They took them, only at least they didn't kill them.
They gave them hard work, and they came back.
All of us Jewish girls, what we was hidden.
We were safe.
We was contacting each other.
We send them parcels to Lindberg for a long time.
We-- I had somewhere a letter from the nun to sending to one of the girls.
She lives in Israel.
They looked for me very much.
They didn't know where I am, because I have been in Israel 1946.
I arrived illegally.
Only they didn't know where I am.
They couldn't find me because a girl changes name after marriage.
And when I find them, I arrived, only a lot of them already passed away.
So they are not alive.
And-- what did I want to say?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes.
So we did try to help them, to support them.
Only we couldn't do much, because that's what happened.
They did get support from us.
Old girls from Israel used to send, to Lindberg, parcels.
And then we didn't get letters anymore from them.
We don't know what happened.
Because the Russian had-- in 1941, religion didn't exist.
It was very forbidden.
Nobody could-- who wants go to church was in-- in problem.
And after the war, we also didn't.
Was very hidden.
I went to the church after the war not-- in a way that nobody would know that I were there.
I went there every Sunday.
I had a lovely priest, and I used to tell him everything what's wrong with me.
What-- I told him the truth, because that was after the war.
I tell him that I have been Jewish and I-- during the war my father put me in.
And I don't know what to do.
I fighting with myself.
I always thought that I will be a nun, because I didn't-- only I can see there is a lot of Jewish people alive.
And I also got, in Argentina, a very big family.
My mother's sister, with kids.
And my daddy wrote everywhere letters that his children are in convent, because he wants to make sure that we'll be back Jewish.
And he told me this.
He used to give me, every Sunday, lollies and nuts.
He was so nice to me.
And he told me that you don't have to be a nun.
You just do what your heart wants.
He was very nice.
Just one day after the church, I went into the orphan.
There were a mixture of children-- Jewish and Germans and Ukrainian, Russian-- everyone what didn't have parents went to that place.
And there was a very big panic.
The-- the children-- the not Jewish children-- had a fight with the Jews.
They make the-- not ammunition, just, like bombs.
Only that wasn't real bombs.
It was like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forgot the name.
Anyway, in that moment, when I came from the church and I saw they start to fight-- no war, and they fight Jewish children-- and my heart start to bleeding.
And I saw that I am Jewish.
I can't be something else.
When they hurt my nation, I was there.
And I went and looked for a way to get out of there, and I want to be what I'm born.
And I-- my-- that Rabbi, Kahene, was working in a synagogue, and he was trying to survive all of us.
And he sent us to Krakow, because they said that the borders will be closed, and we will be never able to leave Russia.
So we tried to get out of Lindberg.
And we-- they sent us-- we were trying to Krakow.
That was a different city.
And-- Approximately when was this?
This was in 1945.
And I went to Krakow, and my sister with me, they-- to a Jewish orphan.
That was international, the other one, and here in Krakow, we are in Jewish orphan.
And my sister was sent to a holiday place-- Zakopane.
That is a special holiday place.
And I was in the orphan.
And they used to come-- the Jewish schlichim used to come and tell us about Israel, because we didn't know much about Israel.
And we knew-- we was praying in convent, about the Jewish people to become all Catholic.
That was the wish of Christian religion.
They wish everyone become Catholic.
Everybody prays differently.
If this is worth, I don't know.
We will see when we die.
The main thing is that I was in that Jewish orphan, and the schlichim did come, and they explained to us Hasidim [?
Chaim, ?] Shomer Hatzair [?
Chaim. ?] And I like the Shomer Hatzair idea because that is-- they like everybody.
They like mixture.
And I like mixture.
I don't like only one, because everybody is nice.
There is Jewish not nice people.
There is Polish not nice.
And there is nice.
We are in a society.
We must trust each other.
And that's how I've taught my children.
And that's how I will die.
I love people.
For me, no difference.
Just to be a mensch, a person.
And I become Shomer Hatzair, and I worked very nice in Krakow.
We built kibbutzim-- you know what is kibbutz?
And we'd send them to Germany.
We were-- because we was not allowed to travel to Israel, because we need quotas.
So we had-- we were sending with boats, like
And that was very tough traveling.
I went with-- mine ship was I was travel-- first my sister-- sorry, I had to interrupt.
My sister got sick.
She had earache, very bad ears problem.
She had to be operated.
And she had to be sent with first Aliyah to Israel.
So she went in 1945, and I had to go-- the first ship what was available, I had to be on it.
I didn't want to go with Aliyah Aleph, because I was very patriotic already.
I am-- with all my heart, if I do something, I like to do it the right way.
And I said, I'd like to go with Aliyah Bet and I want to work for Israel.
And they sent me with Aliyah Bet.
Only before this, I was in Germany.
We built kibbutzim in Krakow, in Poland.
We done brushes, we had a little factory.
We did produce brushes, and we had to sell them.
It was not so easy.
And the money went to a big-- to the-- for-- towards Israel.
The money-- all money is supposed to go there.
And then we were sent to Germans.
In Germany, we-- I was in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jordanbad.
There was a kibbutz looked after also nuns, German nuns.
There was a lot of children, very sick, diabetic.
They had a lot of bad sickness, like syphilis.
A lot of sickness, which we was very frightened.
I didn't know what it is even.
Although I was in a different-- in a healthy group.
I was healthy.
And my sister was with me, and we-- we didn't work that time.
We just eat, and drink, and dance, and a bit of study-- not much.
And we-- our aim was to be in Israel.
We come one day.
We went to Marseilles.
We had to travel with everything hidden, with buses.
We were like-- not human.
All the time hidden, hidden, hidden.
I don't know how it comes that we are still brainy with-- we're working with our brains and we're thinking.
We was traveling in a-- in a big bus.
The bus was locked.
Nobody knew that we are people.
They thought we are some things they're carrying.
We came to friends.
They put us in a home, like a prostitution home, because they had to hide us.
And I looked at the pictures.
I thought, this is angels.
Because I know only angels and devils.
I didn't know prostitution at that time.
And I said, look!
How beautiful, angels!
And this was absolutely naughty pictures.
Any my friends were older than me.
They said, [INAUDIBLE], don't say that!
This is not angels.
This is very bad.
Then I opened my eyes.
I looked again, only I was ashamed to look at it.
It was something terrible.
And all the houses, the rotten places we were put in.
After the war, even, because we was not allowed to travel to-- on land.
And when we went to the ship-- the ship was we had, a day, a tablespoon of water, only chocolate.
And I was so sick.
I asked people to throw me to the beach I don't want to be alive.
I was so sick-- vomiting, vomiting.
We didn't have from what to eat.
That was after the war, when we went through so much problem, we had another war.
And [INAUDIBLE] safely we went to Israel.
Oh, It was long.
Long.
I think a year.
Only four weeks, probably.
I don't know.
I think 80 people.
Yeah.
Maybe 100.
I don't know.
And we were sent to Atlit.
This was a camp from the English people.
They also wasn't such nice people to us.
They wasn't.
They-- we were not-- the boys wasn't allowed to come to us.
We had two groups-- the girls separate and the men separate.
And we had little-- we were sleeping in such a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I forgot what you call it.
And that was our place, every-- three, four girls together.
And we used to study a bit of Hebrew.
They'd give us some lectures, how to behave, and to teach us a bit because we really didn't have much schooling.
And we were hungry of knowledge.
We want to know a lot, and there was not much time for us.
We had to-- we was dancing, we were singing, we was happy.
Because young people go over very fast.
And then one day the boys needs water, they had to come to our camp.
So they was behind the door.
The doors were with opening.
And I ask-- so my husband.
That's how I met my husband.
He wants water, and I took his bucket, a little-- little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cup, and I fill up with water and I give him.
And since then we become closer every time we talked.
And I was sent to Shara [INAUDIBLE] Aliyat Hanoar.
This is for children to-- half day I had to study Hebrew, and half a day I was working in a garden.
And I liked it.
It was nice.
We tried to keep each other.
Till today, when I come to Israel, everybody sees me.
They're very, very close, because we all been without parents and we need each other.
And we are very close.
When I come to Israel, they are making parties for me.
They-- they are sorry that I am not there.
Only my husband didn't want to stay in Israel, because we had two boys in Israel.
And he went through so much.
He said, I don't want my children to go through again this, what I went through.
I want for them a bit of freedom.
And I thought, he's smart.
He knows what he's doing.
And I follow him.
I said, I love Israel.
For me, Israel is very important.
Only when you marry, you have to follow your man.
So I went with him.
I left my sister in Israel.
She was 18.
Yes, she was 18.
She went to the army.
And it was very hard for me to leave her.
She was studying in Israel.
She was in a kibbutz, Merhavia.
And I married in Israel.
In '47. 1947 I was married.
In 1949, I had my first son. 194-- 1952, I had my second son.
In 1955, we left Israel.
And I had here, in 1956, my youngest son.
Three sons, no girls.
That time, I was happy I got boys, because I wanted to be a boy always.
Only I [LAUGHS] sorry I haven't got a daughter.
That's all right.
I've got lovely daughter-in-laws and granddaughters.
You can't pick.
What comes, comes.
They're good children.
They're very good.
They were good students.
They had very clever auntie and uncles, and my father was very-- my mother.
And also, there-- my husband's side.
The kids are good.
When you meet them, you will really like them.
They're lovely children.
They've got feelings.
They-- I'm proud of them.
At least my dad, my mom was praying for me.
I got a lovely family.
That's all what's left.
And I don't mind to do anything for them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I miss that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I haven't got anybody else here, just my children.
Because people-- you can't get much friends.
It's very hard.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's it.
That's my life.
When I arrived to Australia, I left such a lovely country.
Israel was, for me, very important.
I was working very hard.
I had my children.
I learned overlock.
I had three kids.
One was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] four and a half-- five and a half, two and a half, and a baby.
And I had 10 minutes' rest, and I worked on my overlock.
I produced lingerie.
And I helped my husband to save up money to bring the kids on a proper level.
And here was in shortage.
That's a good country.
Australia is a lovely country.
Nice people, lovely people.
Doesn't matter how you speak, they always tell you that you speak beautiful.
They are lovely people.
I really wouldn't change them for nobody else.
The second love what I got is for Australia.
They-- I hope they don't change, they stay like they are, because they are lovely.
I got experience why I say that, because I had a little business.
I was very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] active.
I want to help my family to-- not to be shortage of nothing, to be able to give them what I couldn't get.
And I opened a little shop.
And I couldn't speak English at all.
This is marvelous what I-- because I'm not such talented in languages.
For me, English-- any language to learn is hard.
Just, I am a good businesswoman, and I like people, and I'm honest.
And this is so important, to go your right way.
That's why I never felt this in Europe.
Because I have been honest and I like people.
And people used to come to my shop--
Grocery.
First I start with grocery.
And the names of everything-- I didn't know the names.
I put them all on the shelves.
Only one article, I couldn't put on the shelves-- I had them in storeroom-- and that was eggs.
And when people come for eggs, I didn't know what it is.
And they draw for me a chicken, and oy, God, what I didn't-- Help.
They want to help me.
And they cluck cluck cluck cluck cluck.
And I laughed.
I didn't know what they say.
Only at the end, we come to a conclusion, and I knew they wanted eggs.
They helped me.
They didn't-- they come from everywhere, and they did help me.
They didn't mind that I don't speak, I give them always-- the arithmetic was correct, and if not, I could make a mistake too.
So I give them back.
They come, they support us, they like us.
I-- My first business was in Victoria Street Houghton.
That's why I'm very for Houghton.
I like Houghton.
[LAUGHS] The children like St.
Kilda, you know?
And I like [?
Houghton. ?] This is why I-- and then I had a milk bar in Dickens Street.
With the children, it was very hard.
One of my son had asthma, too.
And it was very hard, because in a milk bar, you have to work from morning till night.
And when he had an attack, it was very hard.
I had to take him to Alfred Hospital.
The doctors were so good, so nice to me.
Really, Australia is something what we have to remember them for all your life.
They helped us.
They support us.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then I decided to have a factory of lingerie, and we opened a factory.
And I was the-- the salesgirl, and I sold to all Australian people.
Used to be Tredway, used to be a lot of businesses.
Today they're called different.
And I used to come to them, and I-- they always tried to help me.
They would-- even Susan Lingerie, which belonged to Jewish people.
[INAUDIBLE].
There was a lady-- Italian lady, only she's, like, Australian-- always gave me orders.
I never came out from a business which I didn't get orders.
And Myer's, they had buyers, Australian buyers.
They were very nice.
